channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Man Who Cuts the Perfect Slice of Ham
item.description: Spaniards line up at weddings, tennis tournaments and galas to eat ham prepared by Ernesto Soriano, one of the world’s best slicers of jamón ibérico.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Gianfranco Tripodo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ernesto Soriano at work inside the Joselito flagship store in Madrid last month. He began learning to carve at 15.

item.title: Red Card or Black Card? The Conscription Lottery in Thailand.
item.description: The draft, which is mandatory for men, is a deeply divisive issue in the country. But volunteer enlistments have soared amid a surge in nationalism and a sluggish economy.
item.dc:creator: Muktita Suhartono and Lauren DeCicca
item.media:description: Volunteer recruits getting fitted for uniforms in Nonthaburi, Thailand, in April. Tens of thousands of men are conscripted every year.

item.title: How China’s Leader Lost Faith in His Generals
item.description: Xi Jinping spent 13 years building a military to rival that of the United States. But the stronger the Chinese forces grew, the less he trusted the generals he had handpicked to run them.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:description: China’s leader, Xi Jinping, during a military parade in Tiananmen Square in 2025 in Beijing.

item.title: Punch, the Lonely Monkey Who Drew Crowds to a Small Japan Zoo, Is Growing Up. He’s Still a Star.
item.description: The macaque, now 9 months old, has been attracting tens of thousands of visitors to a small Japanese zoo. He ignores the attention.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández, Hisako Ueno and Kentaro Takahashi
item.category: People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals
item.media:credit: Kentaro Takahashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Punch has handled the limelight better than most young stars. Since first hitting the headlines he has slowly integrated into his troop and made friends — monkey and human alike.

item.title: Britain’s Electorate Is ‘Splintering.’ Can Its System Stand the Strain?
item.description: Insurgent parties like Reform U.K. surged at the polls this week. But the British electoral system wasn’t built for multiparty democracy.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: “The electorate are fed up with the fact that their lives aren’t changing quickly enough,” Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain said on Friday, after his Labour Party suffered significant losses at the polls.

item.title: A Scaled-Back Victory Day Parade Shows Putin’s Growing Vulnerability in Ukraine War
item.description: The event, shrunken because of security fears, adds to a sense that Moscow and other Russian cities can no longer be insulated from the war.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: Russia’s Victory Day military parade on Saturday in Moscow. The parade, in Red Square, included none of its usual muscle-flexing missiles and armor.

item.title: Rhun ap Iorwerth Breaks Labour’s Dominance in Wales
item.description: Rhun ap Iorwerth, a former journalist, led his party to the most seats in the Welsh Parliament, ousting the Labour Party, which had long held sway, and beating Reform U.K.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Rhun ap Iorwerth, second from left, arriving at a polling station with his wife and daughters in Llangristiolus, Wales, on Thursday.

item.title: Long Overlooked, Caspian Sea Provides Strategic Trade Route for Iran
item.description: The landlocked body of water has taken on new significance, with Russia shipping military and commercial goods to bolster Tehran’s ability to withstand the U.S. assault.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish, Neil MacFarquhar and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A woman walks along the southern coast of the Caspian Sea in the port city of Bandar Anzali, Iran. The Caspian has emerged as a key trade route between Russia and Iran.

item.title: One Nation Wins Australia Special Election, Claiming Seat in Lower House
item.description: One Nation, an anti-immigration party with Trumpian policies that has risen in popularity, won a seat in Parliament’s lower house for the first time.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:credit: Lukas Coch/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: Pauline Hanson, the leader of One Nation, an Australian anti-immigration party.

item.title: In Hungary, Viktor Orban Loyalists Jump Ship
item.description: Peter Magyar, the incoming prime minister, has promised to push out “puppets of the old regime.” Many are jumping first.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Lili Rutai
item.media:description: Peter Magyar, the former opposition leader, last month in Budapest.

item.title: Russia Has Lost More Than 350,000 Soldiers, New Estimate Finds
item.description: The number raises the prospect that about half a million soldiers in total have died on the Russian and Ukrainian sides.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fresh graves of Russian soldiers killed in Ukraine at a cemetery in Vladikavkaz, North Ossetia-Alania, Russia, in February.

item.title: Peter Magyar Takes Over as Hungary’s Leader From Viktor Orban
item.description: The former opposition leader, who won a landslide election, now faces the formidable job of dismantling what his predecessor called an “illiberal democracy.”
item.dc:creator: Lili Rutai
item.media:description: Peter Magyar getting a standing ovation on Saturday in Budapest as he became Hungary’s new prime minister.

item.title: Why the Trump-Xi Summit Matters, Even if Little Seems to Come of It
item.description: The war in Iran, trade, artificial intelligence and Taiwan are expected to be on the agenda. But expectations are modest.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, in Busan, South Korea, in 2025.

item.title: After Frederiksen Falls Short in Denmark, Right-Leaning Politician Is Asked to Form Government
item.description: King Frederik X has appointed a right-leaning politician to try to form the next government.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman and Maya Tekeli
item.media:credit: Emil Nicolai Helms/Ritzau Scanpix, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Given high marks for handling President Trump, Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen of Denmark was seen as falling short when it came to domestic issues.

item.title: U.A.E. Expels Pakistani Workers, as Pakistan’s Peacemaking Creates a Rift
item.description: As Pakistan mediates between the U.S. and Iran, its ties to the Emirates have deteriorated. Pakistani workers say they are now being sent home en masse.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier, Zia ur-Rehman and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Asim Hafeez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Travelers at Pakistan’s Islamabad International Airport this month.

item.title: The French Couple That Tried to Burgle 29 Churches in 3 Months
item.description: A spree of thefts last summer heightened alarm in France over protection of cultural and rural heritage.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter and Ana Castelain
item.media:credit: Andrea Mantovani for The New York Times
item.media:description: The tabernacle in the church of Chamouille, France, which was robbed last summer. Some priests oversee as many as 50 churches.

item.title: Is This Telescope a Threat to U.S. Security?
item.description: Our reporter Emma Bubola visits the foothills of the Argentine Andes to find out why a giant Chinese-sponsored telescope is sitting dismembered.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola, Rebecca Suner, Leila Medina, Sutton Raphael, Sarah Pabst and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: Cruise Ship Linked to Hantavirus Outbreak Arrives in Spain’s Canary Islands for Disembarking
item.description: The MV Hondius, the cruise ship that had carried passengers infected with hantavirus, anchored off the Spanish territory, where other passengers would soon disembark.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Carlos Barragán
item.media:description: The MV Hondius arriving in Tenerife, Canary Islands, on Sunday.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Effectively Blocked After Naval Skirmishes
item.description: The U.S. Navy’s blockade continued to intercept ships trying to leave or enter Iranian ports, while fear of Iranian attacks kept other ships from attempting passage through the narrows.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar and Devon Lum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Tankers anchored in the Strait of Hormuz off the coast of Qeshm Island, Iran, last month.

item.title: Passengers on Hantavirus Cruise Ship Will Disembark in Tenerife and Return to Home Countries
item.description: The cruise ship will arrive on the island of Tenerife, part of the Canary Islands of Spain, officials said. All of the passengers will then be evacuated to their home countries.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald and Carlos Barragán
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Workers on the island of Tenerife setting up tents at an expected reception point for passengers from the cruise ship MV Hondius.

item.title: Deadly Israeli Strikes Erode Cease-Fire in Lebanon
item.description: Fighting between Israel and Hezbollah has shown little sign of abating, with Hezbollah also continuing to attack Israeli troops, and both sides accusing the other of violating a U.S.-brokered truce.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Rescue workers searching for survivors under the rubble of houses damaged by an Israeli airstrike Saturday, in the village of Saksakiyeh in southern Lebanon.

item.title: Why Antiwar Protesters are Rallying in Japan
item.description: Demonstrators are opposed to Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s efforts to build up military power and move the country away from its pacifist identity.
item.dc:creator: Kiuko Notoya and John Yoon
item.media:description: A crowd of protesters in Tokyo this week. One demonstrator holds a number 9, referring to the article of the Japanese constitution that renounces war.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
Early Adopter
item.description: Why does it feel worse to be early than to rush and stress and arrive a little late?
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Canada and the European Union Are in Love. Where Can It Lead?
item.description: A rapid trip with the prime minister to Armenia highlighted the new era of amity between Canada and the European Union. We examine what that can and can’t do for Canadians.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada, left; Mark Rutte, NATO’s secretary general; and Ukraine’s president, Volodymyr Zelensky, at the European Political Community summit in Yerevan. Armenia, on Monday.

item.title: How China May Have Sought to Recruit a House Aide to Spy on the U.S.
item.description: A staff member on the House China Committee was promised $10,000 for U.S. policy insights, on issues like Venezuela and rare-earth minerals.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The outreach appears to provide a vivid portrait of how Beijing’s spy services seek to gain access to information from within the corridors of power in Washington.

item.title: Training For a Marathon in Kenya
item.description: Our reporter John Eligon runs in the Great Rift Valley of Kenya to experience why it’s the mecca of distance running in the world.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon, Christina Thornell, Sutton Raphael, Thomas Vollkommer and Brian Otieno

item.title: China’s Exports and Imports Set Records in April Amid High Energy Costs
item.description: The country’s exports surged and its trade surplus with the United States widened ahead of President Trump’s scheduled visit next week to Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Beijing auto show in April, which featured hundreds of electric cars. Chinese electric vehicles are being exported in record numbers.

item.title: Norovirus Outbreak Sickens 115 on Cruise Ship
item.description: More than 100 passengers and 13 crew members on the Caribbean Princess reported being ill, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: The Caribbean Princess left Port Everglades in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., on April 28 and is expected to return to Florida on Monday.

item.title: U.F.O. Files Released by U.S. Shed Light on What the Government Knows
item.description: The Pentagon on Friday released online what it called “new, never-before-seen” files, dating back decades, related to unidentified flying objects.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: An image of a U.F.O. resembling a football-shaped body near Japan that the U.S. Indo-Pacific Command reported.

item.title: Mysterious American Man Makes Mysterious Proposal in Greenland
item.description: The police in Greenland are investigating the case of a lone man from Las Vegas who goes by Cliff. Locals said he had offered money to Greenlanders to join the United States.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman and Maya Tekeli
item.media:description: Nuuk, Greenland, where a man with an American accent is said to have been asking residents to sign a petition about joining the United States.

item.title: Takeaways From the 2026 U.K. Local Elections
item.description: Results from municipal and regional elections signaled major gains for the right-wing populist Reform U.K. and steep losses for Labour.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: British Prime Minister and Labour leader Keir Starmer in London on Friday.

item.title: There Are Ants in This Canadian Hospital. Again.
item.description: A hospital in Manitoba had to postpone a “limited number of elective surgeries” after ants appeared there for the third time since 2024.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:description: The provincial authority that runs Carman Memorial Hospital in Carman, Manitoba, said it was working with patients to reschedule elective surgeries after ants appeared in the operating room.

item.title: Xia De-hong, 94, Dies; Persecuted in China, She Starred in Daughter’s Memoir
item.description: “Wild Swans,” a best-selling 1991 memoir, told the story of a stoic mother holding her family together amid torture and imprisonment under Mao’s regime.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: Wild Swans: Three Daughters of China (Book)

item.title: In one borough on London’s outer reaches, Conservatives stave off Reform U.K.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Nigel Farage leaving a polling station in eastern England on Thursday.

item.title: Before Hantavirus Outbreak, the MV Hondius Attracted ‘a Different Type of Traveler’
item.description: Former passengers on the MV Hondius say that the ship was made for wildlife expeditions and that the crew took safety very seriously.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Claire Moses and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: The MV Hondius off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday. Passengers on the ship showed symptoms of the hantavirus, and some died.

item.title: Festus Mogae, Former Botswana President Who Tackled H.I.V., Dies at 86
item.description: He led the southern African nation for a decade and embraced a flagship American aid program credited with helping to eradicate the country’s AIDS epidemic.
item.dc:creator: Yvonne Mooka and John Eligon
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:credit: Joao Silva for The New York Times
item.media:description: Festus Mogae in 2004, during his decade as president of Botswana. He fought the country’s H.I.V./AIDS crisis and presided over robust economic growth because of diamond production.

item.title: Oil Slick Is Detected Off Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf
item.description: The slick, captured in satellite images near Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf, has raised concerns about the condition of Iran’s oil infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Mira Rojanasakul and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An image from the European Space Agency’s Copernicus Sentinel-2 satellite showing a slick off Kharg Island on Wednesday.

item.title: Greenpeace Suffers Another Blow in Court Fight With Energy Transfer
item.description: In an unusual move, a North Dakota court said Greenpeace International shouldn’t be allowed to pursue a lawsuit in Europe, where it is based, against the company.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Activists in France protested against the Energy Transfer lawsuit against Greenpeace USA last year.

item.title: Britain Has Entered a New Era of Multiparty Politics. It’s Messy.
item.description: A voting system created when two parties dominated is being tested by the rise of newer competitors.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Voters in North London on Thursday. In Britain’s voting system, the more candidates there are for a council seat, the smaller the share it might take to win.

item.title: Four Men Found Guilty of Haiti President’s Assassination
item.description: President Jovenel Moïse of Haiti was shot in his bedroom in July 2021, but the convictions for conspiracy have not revealed who ordered his assassination.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams and Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural of Jovenel Moïse near his home in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, where he was assassinated in 2021.

item.title: SNP, Scotland’s Pro-Independence Party, Benefits From Labour’s Struggles With Voters
item.description: The unpopularity of Keir Starmer’s party since he became Britain’s prime minister has helped boost the Scottish National Party.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: A campaign event for the Scottish National Party in Dundee, Scotland, in April.

item.title: If Starmer is badly weakened, who could challenge him for Britain’s top job?
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear

item.title: Even in Labour-Loyal Wales, Voters Are Looking to Other Parties
item.description: Opinion polls before the election suggested that the right-wing anti-immigration Reform U.K. party and the center-left Plaid Cymru would battle for the top spot in Wales.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Canvassers for Plaid Cymru, one of the parties trying to defeat Labour in Wales in Tredegar, last month.

item.title: The Green Party gained ground in some of Labour’s London strongholds.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: A Green Party candidate won the mayor’s race in the London borough of Hackney.

item.title: Two Men Are Convicted of Spying for China in Britain
item.description: The men were arrested in 2024, accused of surveilling dissidents from Hong Kong in Britain. The case sent a chill through pro-democracy protesters who have fled the territory.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Chung Biu Yuen, left, and Chi Leung “Peter” Wai, right, arriving ahead of their trial at the Old Bailey in central London in March.

item.title: What’s at Stake in the UK’s Local Elections
item.description: The voting in England, Scotland and Wales is the biggest electoral test for Keir Starmer since he became prime minister in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain spoke on Friday after local election results began rolling in.

item.title: Farage Celebrates Reform UK’s Strong Performance in Local Elections
item.description: In early results, Nigel Farage’s Reform U.K. party has won more than 400 council races across England.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Nigel Farage, Britain’s Reform U.K. leader, spoke to the media in the London Borough of Havering, Britain, on Friday.

item.title: Emirates Doubles Down on U.S. and Israeli Ties Amid Iran War
item.description: The United Arab Emirates has borne the brunt of Iranian attacks throughout the war, hardening the government’s resolve to reassess its list of friends and foes.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Emirates leader, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed, and President Trump in Abu Dhabi, last year.

item.title: Taiwan Breaks Political Deadlock to Approve $25 Billion U.S. Arms Purchase
item.description: With time running out, Taiwanese lawmakers approved funds for buying U.S. weapons, fearing they could lose support if they didn’t act now.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:description: A Patriot missile system deployed at a park in Taipei during a military exercise last year. The Patriot is one of the most sought-after air-defense systems on the weapons market.

item.title: Court Revives ‘Farmgate’ Case Against South African President
item.description: Judges ordered Parliament to convene a committee to consider removing President Cyril Ramaphosa over a case involving money stolen at his farm six years ago.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon
item.media:description: At the Constitutional Court in Johannesburg on Friday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi, Erica L. Green, Motoko Rich and Euan Ward

item.title: 3 Hikers Dead After Volcano Erupts in Indonesia
item.description: Indonesian authorities said other climbers had safely evacuated Mount Dukono, which had been off-limits because of volcanic activity.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Muktita Suhartono
item.media:credit: Indonesia's Geological Agency, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Smoke rises above Mount Dukono in Indonesia on Friday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear

item.title: China Sees a ‘Giant With a Limp’ as U.S. Drains Weapons on Iran War
item.description: America’s ability to deter China in a war over Taiwan is weakened, Chinese analysts say, giving Beijing leverage in an upcoming summit with President Trump.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Berry Wang
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Center for Strategic and International Studies
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, in Busan, South Korea, in October. Both sides agreed to extend a trade truce to limit tariffs.

item.title: Many Russians in No Mood for Celebration on Kremlin’s Biggest Day of the Year
item.description: As the country heads into Victory Day, the economy is stalling, internet restrictions are growing and the Kremlin is fearing Ukrainian attacks.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Nanna Heitmann
item.category: Communist Party of the Russian Federation
item.media:description: Red Square in Moscow. The Victory Day procession, one of the year’s biggest events, is staged in the heart of Kremlin power.

item.title: U.S. to Review Mexican Consulates After Right-Wing Claims Against Them
item.description: The State Department said it would review all Mexican consulates in the United States, following claims in conservative media that the consulates interfere in politics.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Alisha Jucevic for The New York Times
item.media:description: The State Department is reviewing the 53 Mexican consulates in the U.S., including the consulate in Los Angeles.
